name: Inbox Agent
body: Leads go cold in hours, and they always land while you're mid-event or asleep. Inbox Agent reads every inbound inquiry, classifies it, and drafts a response in your voice, trained on __3+ years of sent email__.
You wake up to replies waiting for approval instead of an inbox full of guilt. Nothing sits, nothing slips.

name: Morning Brief
body: Most operators start the day with an hour of tab-checking: email, calendar, CRM, socials. That hour is gone before real work starts.
The brief lands in Slack at 06:00 with new leads ranked by score, overdue tasks, upcoming events, and *one priority action*. The day starts decided, not discovered.

name: Teach — Coach Feedback Loop
body: Every automation drifts: it guesses wrong, you correct it, and next week it makes the same mistake. Corrections that don't stick are wasted breath.

Teach feeds the orchestrator agent's memory directly. He queues his biggest open questions, you answer once, and the answer becomes __permanent operating knowledge__. Errors drop, drift stops, operations keep flowing.

name: Post-Event Pipeline
body: The day after an event is admin quicksand: download the archive, upload to Drive, dig up gallery links, write the thank-you. It's the work you're too tired to do well.
This runs the whole sequence automatically: media archived, project record updated with gallery links and stats, thank-you email drafted. You sleep in; the client still gets same-day polish.

name: Design Request Pipeline
body: Design briefs assembled over eight back-and-forth emails burn days and client goodwill, and the designer still starts with half the assets missing.
This scans email for brand assets, builds a *structured brief*, links everything to Drive, and queues it complete. The designer opens a finished brief every single time.

name: OSINT Research Pipeline
body: Big accounts don't go to the cheapest booth; they go to the vendor who *understood the account*. Walking into a pitch blind is how you stay a commodity.

This runs deep research on the client, their company, and the venue before you ever reply: who decides, what they've booked, what they care about. It builds the Notion records and generates a tailored pitch design request. Knowledge is what lands the larger accounts.

name: New Client Buildout
body: Every booking kicks off an hour of setup drudgery: records, folders, checklists, links. Skip a step and it bites you the week of the event.

One command builds the full event infrastructure in Notion: Client, Company, Venue, Design Request, Event Details, and Project records, all cross-linked, plus the Google Drive folder and equipment checklist. Booked to fully-staged in minutes.

name: Lead Score Agent
body: Treating every inquiry equally means the $5k corporate lead waits in line behind a price shopper. First-come-first-served is a terrible sales strategy.
Every inquiry gets scored across __13 weighted signals__: event proximity, guest count, upsell potential, repeat-client flags, price sensitivity. The hot ones surface before you even open Gmail.

name: Gmail Lead Scanner
body: A missed lead doesn't complain; it just books your competitor. You never find out what that buried email cost you.
The scanner watches the inbox for booth inquiries on a schedule and flags every one for follow-up. Missing a lead becomes nearly impossible.

name: SEO Agent
body: Rankings slip quietly, and by the time bookings dip it's been months. Paid ads are the expensive apology for not watching.
This monitors booth-rental keywords across local markets and surfaces drops and competitor gains before they compound. It's the system behind a __page-1 position with zero ad spend__.

name: Competitor Intel Engine
body: Pricing blind against local competitors costs you one of two things: margin you didn't need to give up, or deals you didn't need to lose.
Passive OSINT tracks every area competitor: pricing, venue relationships, social velocity, review signals, all on a color-coded service-area map. You quote knowing the field.

name: Blog Agent
body: Content marketing works, and nobody running events has time to do it. The blog that was going to bring in organic leads has three posts from two years ago.
This researches, outlines, writes, and formats SEO-optimized industry articles with matched cover images, on a schedule. The content engine runs whether you're busy or not.

name: Penny — Finance Platform
body: Booth money is messy: deposits, balances, contractor pay, gear costs, all scattered across apps. Tax season becomes archaeology.
Penny is the money brain: ledger, invoices, payment tracking, and QuickBooks dual-write in one platform. Every dollar in and out is *queryable in seconds*.

name: Gift Card Generator
body: Discounting to close a deal trains clients to expect discounts. You win the booking and cheapen the brand in the same move.
Branded gift cards with tracked codes close the same deals while *protecting your list price*. The client feels they won something; your pricing integrity survives.

name: ACH Info Sender
body: Corporate clients want to pay by bank transfer, and retyping account details into email is both tedious and one typo away from a payment lost in limbo.
One step packages and sends verified payment instructions. Fast for them, safe for you.

name: COI Creator
body: "The venue needs a COI by Friday" usually means days of waiting on an insurance agent while your load-in approval hangs in the balance.
This generates the ACORD certificate with additional-insured language and sends it in minutes. Venue requirement handled before the call ends.

name: W9 Generator
body: Corporate procurement won't cut a check until the W-9 clears, and every day it sits unfiled is a day your payment doesn't move.
One command generates the pre-filled, signed W-9 package and delivers it. Vendor onboarding stops stalling your cash flow.

name: Invoice PDF Fetcher
body: "Can you resend my invoice?" arrives at the worst possible moment, every time, and hunting through the billing system mid-event is nobody's idea of service.
This pulls a clean PDF invoice for any client or event, booked or pre-payment lead, in about 30 seconds.

name: Event Details Builder
body: Event details scattered across email threads guarantee a day-of surprise: wrong load-in door, missing timeline, unassigned staff.
This pulls CRM data and builds a fully structured Event Details page in one shot: timeline, venue logistics, equipment list, design brief, staff assignments. Everything the event needs, one page.

name: Event Logistics Analyzer
body: Double-booking Saturdays is where booth businesses either grow or implode. Guessing drive times is how you end up apologizing to a bride.
This calculates drive times with *real traffic buffers*, builds staff schedules, checks calendar conflicts, and scores multi-event feasibility. "Can we pull off both?" gets answered with data.

name: Equipment Checklist Printer
body: Every operator has left something at a venue: a cable, a light, once in a while the printer. The 11pm teardown is where gear goes to disappear.
This prints any equipment checklist as a clean PDF grouped by case and bag, with charge-after flags and pocket tags. Pack-out and pack-away run off the same sheet.

name: Event Folder Builder
body: "It's somewhere in Drive" is a sentence that costs minutes daily and hours weekly. Inconsistent folders make every handoff a scavenger hunt.
This creates the same standardized Drive tree for every event: design files, client previews, event output, admin docs. Anyone on the team finds anything, instantly.

name: Drive Event Dedup
body: Duplicate event folders breed silently until nobody knows which copy holds the real files. Cleaning it up by hand risks deleting the wrong one.
This finds duplicates by date and fuzzy event name, then archives confirmed dupes safely. It *never deletes*. Years of event storage, kept clean.

name: Event Staffing Agent
body: Staffing a month of events means a week of group texts, no-replies, and double-checking three calendars. It's the chore that never ends.
This discovers upcoming events that need staff, cross-verifies across Notion, Calendar, and Gmail, then sends personalized briefing texts to your chosen people. A month staffed in one sitting.

name: Staff Briefing Sheets
body: Staff who show up under-briefed make the brand look improvised, and no one reads a forwarded email chain in the parking lot.
This generates a *one-page PDF briefing* per person per event: timeline, venue, contacts, setup notes. Everyone walks in knowing exactly what the day looks like.

name: Staff Dispatch
body: Who-goes-where lives in your head until the weekend two events collide and it doesn't. Verbal assignments evaporate.
Dispatch coordinates assignments across the event calendar and gets each person their marching orders. Pairs with the briefing sheets so dispatch and prep happen together.

name: 360-Music-Box
body: 360 videos live or die on the track, and hunting music one download at a time wastes the subscription you're already paying for.

This scrapes daily on autopilot, squeezing __maximum value from the subscription of your choice__, filing everything into an organized Google Drive plus a Notion/Excel sheet for agent progress tracking.
The built-in audio trimmer adds fade effects and exports in every format your booth software wants. Every 360 video ships with a clean, event-ready track.

name: Watermark App
body: Unbranded gallery photos are free advertising you never collect on, and hand-watermarking hundreds of files after midnight is not a marketing plan.
This batch-marks everything with real controls: positioning, opacity, per-batch presets. Multi-format export including *compressed WebP* keeps galleries fast while every share carries your brand.

name: Snappic Microsite Builder
body: The default guest gallery looks the same for every operator in town. Your best branding moment, the link every guest opens, shows up generic.
This generates custom CSS per event, color-matched to the design package. Guests open a *branded experience*, and corporate clients notice the difference.

name: Template Resource Scraper
body: Building a template library one manual download at a time means your design options stay thin and your late nights stay long.

This automated scraper collects industry template resources (welcome screens, print overlays, 360 overlays, PSD templates) into an organized asset library, deduplicated against what you already own. The library grows itself.

name: Property Return
body: Gear left at a venue usually stays lost. Whoever finds it has no idea whose it is or how to reach you, so a $2,000 kit becomes someone's shelf decoration.

Every piece of equipment carries a QR code. A finder scans it, lands on a branded found-property page, and you get an __instant alert with the scanner's location__. They get a contact form with photo upload, routed to your email and SMS. Lost gear finds its way home.

name: Client Montage Builder
body: After the event, the client's excitement peaks and then fades daily. A highlight video is the perfect strike-while-hot upsell, except editing one takes a night you don't have.

This builds a branded montage from the event's photos automatically: intro, crossfades, end card, music. Renders in minutes, *prices like an add-on*, and lands while the event is still the talk of their office.

name: Analytics Report — Booth.Events
body: Corporate clients spend five figures on activations and get back a link and a shrug. When budget season comes, the booth is the line item nobody can defend.

This turns Booth.Events gallery data into a deliverable that defends the spend: __branded cover, executive-level briefing__, engagement and share activity, peak-time analysis, and a contacts CSV. Computer vision picks the *highlight photos of the event*, a QR code links straight to the gallery, and a branded final page carries the rebooking CTA.
It performs better than what either booth software company offers out of the box. Guaranteed to blow your corporate clients away and set you apart from your competition.
Not for sale in DFW :)

name: Gallery Export — Snappic
body: Pulling the full photo and GIF archive out of the Snappic dashboard after every event is click-click-click busywork, and skipping it means client media lives on someone else's servers.
This downloads the complete event zip and files it straight into the event's Drive folder automatically. Every gallery archived, owned, and organized without touching the dashboard.

name: Gallery Export — Booth.Events
body: Every Booth.Events gallery you don't archive is client media you don't control. The manual download-and-file routine is exactly the chore that gets skipped on busy weeks.
This grabs the full event media zip, uploads it to the event's Drive folder, and captures the public gallery URL for the post-event email. Delivery-ready the morning after.

name: Video-to-Website
body: Video on a website usually just sits in a player, ignored. All that production value, one thumbnail deep.

This turns video into the site itself: *parallax scroll effects that intertwine scrolling with video playback*. Exploding objects, before/after transformations, diagram pop-outs. The visitor's scroll drives the story, and nobody clicks away mid-scroll.
